briefing.opening: Good morning, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for June 12, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis: Global Impacts and Warnings
sections.content: According to recent research, global warming is projected to reach 1.37 degrees Celsius by 2025 due to human activities [82], and the official arrival of a powerful Super El Niño has been confirmed, foreshadowing the impacts of future climate change [79, 96]. These climate changes are already directly impacting food security, such as doubling tomato prices in North America [2], and a tragic event occurred where 7% of the population of the world's rarest species, the Tapanuli orangutan, was wiped out by just four days of heavy rainfall [13]. Furthermore, threats to social infrastructure and ecosystems are becoming a reality, with 1.8 million homes in the UK at risk of subsidence and a surge in coastal flood risks [3, 78]. Scientists point out that current climate policies focusing solely on carbon dioxide are insufficient and should include indirect greenhouse gases, and some countries are even showing moves to dismantle climate change and ocean monitoring systems, once again reminding us of the importance of climate action [7, 72].

sections.title: 🌍 Progress in International Cooperation and Climate Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: International cooperation to address the climate crisis continues steadily. The Republic of Korea held its first joint committee for climate change cooperation with Peru to discuss greenhouse gas reduction projects [1, 4, 5, 6, 10], and also agreed with Italy to expand cooperation in climate change, alongside AI semiconductors and biotechnology [8]. Following the energy crisis originating from the Middle East, Korea and the European Union (EU) are significantly strengthening cooperation on renewable energy transition, and discussions on renewable energy transition are spreading in the ASEAN region amidst supply chain instability [69, 70]. Furthermore, African nations are receiving assistance in resolving resource conflicts through climate adaptation [75], and Malaysia is exploring innovative climate adaptation measures, such as cultured meat, to address the arrival of El Niño and food insecurity [83].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Expansion of Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Community Participation
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are being made across communities to transition to a carbon-neutral society. Cheongju City encouraged citizen participation by providing a total of 120 million KRW in carbon neutrality point incentives to approximately 8,000 households that practiced energy saving [15, 30, 33, 36, 47, 49, 51]. Seoul Metropolitan Government and Seoul Energy Corporation are extensively operating carbon neutrality practice campaigns through bicycle commuting [20, 26, 27, 28, 35, 37, 43, 46], and participation in the education sector is also active, with 542 schools in Seoul voluntarily engaging in carbon neutrality practices [29]. Additionally, the Rural Development Administration successfully commercialized native microorganisms [11], resource security and carbon neutrality are being strengthened through the recycling of discarded telecommunications equipment [21, 25], and daily life practice activities such as 'plogging' and 'children's farmer experience' by local residents' self-governing committees are spreading nationwide [24, 45, 50]. In the latter half of the 22nd National Assembly, a special committee on climate will be formed, and the revision of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act will be re-promoted, so policy support is also expected to be strengthened [17].

sections.title: ⚠️ Domestic and International Renewable Energy Transition: Challenges and Opportunities
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is a global challenge, but it still presents many challenges and opportunities simultaneously. Domestically, difficulties are being reported in various places regarding the achievement of the 100GW renewable energy target, and questions are being raised about the approach of leaving the renewable energy transition solely to the market [52, 53, 54]. Furthermore, the economic issue of renewable energy being more expensive than fossil fuels, despite solar and wind being 'free,' is still being discussed [63]. Nevertheless, positive progress is also being observed. In the UK, issues with renewable energy grid connection have begun to be resolved [55], and South Africa's state-owned power utility has launched a dedicated organization for renewable energy projects [64]. Domestically, investments to expand renewable energy infrastructure are also active, with Pacifico Energy Korea pursuing the Jindo offshore wind project with domestic capital and establishing stable financial partnerships [59, 61, 65].

briefing.closing: As we have reviewed in today's briefing, the climate crisis is a global challenge, but at the same time, various international collaborations and domestic and international efforts to overcome it are continuously underway. This concludes the climate briefing for June 12, 2026.

summary.phenomenon: On an unspecified Thursday, plans for a new Natural History GCSE course were announced in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. This course aims to teach students how to cultivate wildflower-friendly gardens.
summary.cause: This course is the result of a movement that has advocated for over a decade to introduce the study of biodiversity loss and global warming as a dedicated subject. It aims to explore human impacts on the natural world and identify everyday methods to aid biodiversity.
summary.outlook: Through this course, students will learn practical ways to support biodiversity.
